A total of at least two peaks and two valleys must be identifiable anywhere within the formation

rule_text: BB (Bulkowski pattern): Tall patterns perform better than short ones. Calculate pattern height by dividing the difference between high and low by the breakout price; if result exceeds median, pattern is classified as tall.

rule_text: Broadening Formation: Buy at the low after stock makes its turn at the lower trend line; Sell short after prices start heading down from the top trend line
entry_conditions: Once broadening formation is recognized
Buy after the stock makes its turn at the lower trend line (for long trades)
Sell short after prices start heading down from the top trend line (for short trades)
exit_conditions: Long stop: Place stop-loss order 0.15 below the minor low
Short stop: Place stop 0.15 above the minor high; Cover short when price turns at bottom trend line and starts moving up; For downward breakout, cover as it nears target price or any support level
Move stops: Raise or lower the stop to the next closest minor low or high once prices make a new high (for long trades) or low (for short sales)

rule_text: Trade patterns based on height and width characteristics relative to median values and market conditions
entry_conditions: Calculate pattern height: (highest high in pattern - lowest low in pattern) / breakout price
Compare height to median: values above median = tall; values below median = short
Calculate pattern width: measure pattern length in time
Compare width to median length: above median = wide; below median = narrow
In bull markets, prefer patterns that are both tall AND narrow
In bear markets, prefer patterns that are tall (width preference stated as incomplete in excerpt)

rule_text: BARR (Bar) Measure Rule: A method to predict minimum price decline by calculating lead-in height from the first quarter of the formation and subtracting from the breakout point.
entry_conditions: BARR formation identified with trend line established
Breakout occurs with trend line pierced heading down
Lead-in height calculated from highest high to trend line in first quarter of formation, measured vertically or as widest distance between the two
exit_conditions: Target price = Trend line piercing point (breakout point) minus Lead-in height
Example: If lead-in height is 3.50 and trend line is pierced at 25.13, target price is 21.63 (25.13 - 3.50)

Eve bottom: wide, rounded formation with multiple short spikes
Combination of Adam and Eve double bottom patterns

rule_text: Adam & Eve Double Bottom: A pattern consisting of two bottoms, with the first bottom (Adam) being narrow and deep (like an oblong hole), and the second bottom (Eve) being wider and shallower (like an excavated pit). The pattern forms in a bull market context.
entry_conditions: First bottom (Adam) appears as a narrow, deep depression
Second bottom (Eve) appears as a wider, shallower depression
Pattern forms during a bull market
Breakout occurs above the confirmation line

Identification Guidelines
How do you identify an Adam & Eve double bottom? First, let us look at a few
examples. Figure 14.2 shows an AEDB midway up a price trend that began in
March 2003. I show this
rule_text: Adam & Eve Double Bottom (AEDB): A corrective pattern consisting of two bottoms where the Adam bottom is a one-day downward price spike, and the Eve bottom has a shorter spike with nearby wide price congestion
entry_conditions: Adam bottom appears as a one-day downward price spike
Eve bottom follows with a spike that is shorter than Adam bottom
Eve bottom is differentiated by nearby wide price congestion (versus narrower congestion at Adam bottom)
Pattern can occur midway in uptrend or within longer-term downtrend

Prices move decidedly above the neckline with a definitive breakout
Volume advances noticeably after breakout (may occur with a lag of up to 2 weeks)

rule_text: Head-and-Shoulders Bottom (Inverse H&S): A three-trough formation with the center trough significantly below the other two outer troughs, creating an inverted head-and-shoulders pattern.
entry_conditions: Three-trough formation with center trough below the other two
Left shoulder and right shoulder at approximately the same price level
Left and right shoulders appear to be about the same width
Symmetry common in head-and-shoulders formations

Head is below both shoulders by a reasonable amount (distinguishes from triple bottom)
Three troughs and two minor rises should appear well defined

rule_text: Heads-and-Shoulders Bottom: A reversal pattern with three troughs (left shoulder, head, right shoulder) and two minor rises between them, characterized by symmetry about the head and a neckline connecting the rises between shoulders.
entry_conditions: Three troughs well defined with two minor rises between them
Left and right shoulders opposite one another about the head, somewhat equidistant in both time and price

For up-sloping necklines, use highest high in pattern as breakout price
Volume usually highest on left shoulder or head and diminished on right shoulder

rule_text: Pattern name: Dual-Shoulder Complex Head-and-Shoulders Bottom. A head-and-shoulders bottom formation with two pairs of shoulders (four shoulders total) and a single head, where the left and right outer shoulders are near the same price level, and the head is below the shoulders.
entry_conditions: Formation consists of dual shoulders (two left shoulders, one head, two right shoulders)
Left and right outer shoulders are well-defined and positioned near the same price level
Head is positioned below both the left and right shoulders
Horizontal neckline present
Throwback to neckline occurs after breakout
Pattern is identifiable as a complex variant of normal head-and-shoulders bottoms

Volume pattern heavier on right than on left (atypical)

Rounding bottom characteristics visible
Throwback to neckline occurs (common for head-and-shoulders family, especially complex variety)

rule_text: Head-and-Shoulders Top: A reversal pattern with three peaks where the center peak (head) towers above two surrounding peaks (shoulders), connected by a trend line (neckline) drawn along the bottoms of the two troughs between the three peaks.
entry_conditions: Three peaks with center peak higher than both flanking peaks
Trend line drawn along bottoms of two troughs between the three peaks forms the neckline
Neckline may slope in any direction
Price breaks below the neckline
exit_conditions: Neckline slope direction is a predictor of the severity of price decline

rule_text: Head-and-Shoulders Top Pattern: A reversal pattern characterized by a left shoulder, head (highest point), right shoulder, and neckline support level, with volume highest on the left shoulder formation and weakest on the right shoulder
entry_conditions: Formation of left shoulder at resistance
Head rises above left shoulder
Right shoulder forms, typically lower than the head
Neckline connects the valleys between shoulders and head
Volume highest on formation of left shoulder
Volume weakest on formation of right shoulder
Stock price declines and breaks below neckline support level

rule_text: Head-and-Shoulders Top: A reversal pattern where price climbs to a right shoulder on weak volume, then declines through a neckline support level with volume confirmation

exit_conditions: Price target: decline back to where pattern began (AUTHOR_SILENT on formal target computation)

rule_text: Head and Shoulders Top (HST): A reversal pattern where price rises to a peak (left shoulder), declines, rises again to a higher peak (head), declines again, then rises to a lower peak (right shoulder) before breaking down below the neckline.
entry_conditions: Price forms left shoulder peak
Price declines from left shoulder
Price rises to higher head peak
Price declines from head
Price rises to lower right shoulder peak
Price breaks below the neckline (support level connecting the two valley lows)
Pattern should reside in middle or upper third of yearly price range to have something to reverse
exit_conditions: Measure rule: Price typically declines by the distance from the head peak to the neckline, projected downward from the breakout point
Author caution: Set price target closer than measure rule suggests, as 54% of HSTs in bull markets fail to drop at least 20%

rule_text: Island patterns: Tall and narrow islands outperform other combinations in bear markets with downward breakouts. Tall and wide islands perform best in bull markets with upward breakouts. Islands get progressively shorter as they approach the end of a trend.
entry_conditions: Identify island pattern formation
Classify island as tall/narrow or tall/wide
Confirm market direction (bull or bear)
Confirm breakout direction (up or down)
Note island position in trend sequence (first, second, third, etc.)
Observe that islands decrease in width as trend nears completion

parameters.island_width_characteristic: Islands get shorter as they progress toward end of trend

rule_text: Islands pattern: A reversal or continuation pattern identified by a gap separating price action, with a second gap closing the island formation
entry_conditions: Formation of an island created by two gaps in opposite directions
Island appears well into the trend (avoid unusually narrow islands as they may signal trend change)
Trade in the direction of the general market and other stocks in the sector
exit_conditions: Target price computation: Measure rule - Calculate formation height by subtracting the lowest low from the highest high in the pattern. Divide the height by 2. Apply this result to the closing price the day before the second gap. For downward breakouts, subtract the result from the closing price to obtain target price. Example: Formation height (29.75 - 20 = 9.75), divided by 2 = 4.88. Target = 25.99 - 4.88 = 21.11

rule_text: MMD (Multiple Moving Down) Pattern: A bearish pattern where price declines in a first leg, retraces in a corrective phase, then declines again in a second leg. Target is projected by measuring the first leg decline and projecting downward.

Corrective phase retraces a portion of the first leg
Second leg decline begins after corrective phase completes

exit_conditions: Target calculation: Project first leg move downward to hit target between 83% and 93% of the time
target_regimes: BEAR_MARKET - decline is steeper and price travels farther in bear market than bull market

rule_text: Measured Move Down: A two-legged decline where the slope of the trend line is similar for both legs, preceded by a corrective phase between the legs. A decline that appears to end (first leg) is followed by a corrective bounce (corrective phase), then a second leg down of similar magnitude.

Second leg down begins from correction point (C)
The slope of the trend line is similar for both the first leg and second leg
Pattern forms after a long decline
exit_conditions: Target is measured by the length of the first leg (A to B), then projected downward from the corrective phase high point (C)

rule_text: Measured Move Down: A decline from a new high that forms a price peak, followed by a straight-line run lower forming a down-sloping trend channel, then a corrective phase retracing 38-62% of losses.
entry_conditions: Prices reach a new high and trend change begins
Prices decline, leaving a price peak on the chart
Prices continue moving lower, usually in a straight-line run
Two parallel lines can be drawn: one connecting minor highs and one joining minor lows, forming a down-sloping trend channel
Corrective phase stops the decline with prices moving horizontally or retracing losses

First leg Usually begins from a new high. Prices decline rapidly in a straight-
line fashion. Avoid declines that curve (they are rounding turns,
scallops, or saucers).
Corre
rule_text: Measured Move pattern: A sharp decline (first leg) followed by a corrective recovery phase, then resumption of the downtrend (second leg)
entry_conditions: First leg: prices decline rapidly in straight-line fashion from a new high
First leg: avoid declines that curve (rounding turns, scallops, or saucers)
Corrective phase: prices move horizontally or rise, recovering 38% to 62% of the prior decline
Corrective phase: if it nears or rises above the first leg high, reject the pattern
Second leg: slope of first leg down trend line often carries onto this leg
Both legs: usually fit inside their own trend channels
exit_conditions: Avoid cascading measured moves: use the first retrace, not later ones as they get progressively closer to the end of the trend
Avoid horizontal, saw-tooth consolidation regions and measured moves

rule_text: Measured Move Down: A two-leg decline pattern where prices fall, consolidate horizontally in a saw-tooth pattern, then resume the downtrend in a second leg that approximates the first leg's decline in both magnitude and time duration.
entry_conditions: First leg: Initial price decline establishing a downtrend slope
Consolidation phase: Horizontal, saw-tooth consolidation regions following the first leg decline
Second leg: Downturn resumes after consolidation
Second leg approximates the price decline set by the first leg
Second leg approximates the time duration of the first leg
Second leg usually fits inside its own trend channel with straight-line fashion decline
Avoid formations that show a rounded first leg
exit_conditions: Measure rule: Target price calculated from first leg decline applied to consolidation base (ensure measure does not predict prices below zero)

rule_text: Measured Move Down: Pattern formed by a straight-line decline (first leg) followed by a corrective phase bounce, then a measured move down to a target level equal to the first leg decline distance
entry_conditions: First leg must be a straight-line decline (avoid rounded/saucer-shaped declines)
Corrective phase bounce should not rebound to or near the high set by the first leg
If prices near or rise above the first leg high, avoid the formation
Trade on the first or second measured move in a declining trend; avoid subsequent moves
Pattern may occur in horizontal trending conditions where initial bounce could be misinterpreted
exit_conditions: Target: Measured move down equals the distance of the first leg decline

rule_text: Measured Move Down: A two-leg price pattern where prices decline, consolidate at a base with horizontal saw-tooth bounces, then resume downward movement. The second leg approximates the price move of the first leg.
entry_conditions: First leg: sustained price decline from an initial high
Consolidation phase: prices bounce at base multiple times creating horizontal saw-tooth formation
Second leg: prices return to base and begin sustained move downward
Measure rule: second leg decline should approximate the magnitude of the first leg decline
Avoid measured moves that spring from a horizontal trend
exit_conditions: Target price: second leg decline should approximate first leg decline magnitude (measure rule)

rule_text: Measured Move Down: A correction pattern following a long price rise, where prices decline along a down trend line within a trend channel, followed by a corrective phase on volume

rule_text: Measured Move: A two-leg price movement pattern where prices retrace a decline, then tumble again in a second leg that is steeper and shorter in duration than the first leg, followed by another measured move that ends at approximately the same level as the first formation
entry_conditions: First leg: prices decline from initial high
Corrective phase: prices retrace 68% of the decline

Second leg is steeper than the first leg
Second leg covers the distance in approximately half the time of the first leg
Volume begins on high but not unusually high levels

rule_text: Rectangle pattern: Price oscillates between a top trend line and bottom trend line, creating a rectangular formation. Breakout occurs when price closes beyond either the top or bottom trend line.
entry_conditions: Top trend line identified (e.g., 12.44 in example)
Bottom trend line identified (e.g., 11.00 in example)
Price oscillates between these two levels forming a rectangle
Breakout occurs above top trend line or below bottom trend line
exit_conditions: Upward breakout target: Add formation height to top trend line (example: 12.44 + 1.44 = 13.88)
Downward breakout target: Subtract formation height from bottom trend line (example: 11.00 - 1.44 = 9.56)
Alternative measure rule (vertical): Measure length of rectangle, flip vertically, add/subtract from trend line
Vertical measure rule works between 50% and 66% (success rate stated with caution)

rule_text: Rectangle top: Prices consolidate between overhead resistance and support levels, with horizontal trend lines connecting minor highs and lows, followed by an upward breakout
entry_conditions: Prices consolidate for over a month

Early price overshoots above and below trend lines are acceptable if they occur early in pattern formation
Breakout occurs upward from the rectangle

rule_text: Rectangle Top: A consolidation pattern where price bounces between horizontal resistance (top trend line) and support (bottom trend line) before breaking out in either direction.
entry_conditions: Price oscillates between a horizontal resistance level (peaks) and horizontal support level (valleys)
Horizontal trend lines can be drawn along the peaks and valleys
The pattern forms over time as buyers and sellers reach temporary equilibrium
A breakout occurs when either the resistance or support level is pierced

rule_text: Scallop pattern: When stock breaks out in one direction and then reverses, trade the new direction. If stock breaks out downward, drops a bit, and then recovers to close above the left scallop high (the lower of the two scallop peaks), buy the stock.
entry_conditions: Stock breaks out in one direction
Stock reverses direction after initial breakout
For upward trade signal: stock breaks downward, drops a bit, then recovers to close above the left scallop high (the lower of the two scallop peaks)
Small move (5% or less) in breakout direction precedes large rebound in opposite direction (busted pattern definition)
exit_conditions: Use stops to protect profits and limit losses

rule_text: Ascending Scallop: A pattern where price forms a bowl-shaped formation with a right side high, used to determine minimum expected price target through the measure rule
entry_conditions: Formation completes with a breakout from the ascending scallop pattern
Right side of formation establishes a high point
Bowl formation with lowest low point identified
exit_conditions: Target price calculation: Subtract the lowest low reached in the bowl from the high reached on the right side of the formation to determine height. Add this height value to the highest high on the right side (for upward breakouts) or subtract it from the lowest low (for downward breakouts). The result is the minimum expected price target.

rule_text: Scallop pattern: A formation with a base low (point B) and right side high (point A), used for measuring price targets via the measure rule
entry_conditions: Scallop formation completes with identifiable base low (point B)

Formation creates a bowl-like structure
exit_conditions: Upward breakout target: Add formation height (right side high minus base low) to right side high (point A)
Downward breakout target: Subtract formation height from formation low (point B)
Example: Base low 12.50, Right side high 16.00, Formation height 3.50, Upward target 19.50; Downward target 9.00

rule_text: Ascending Scallop: A pattern where prices form a scallop formation with an ascending right edge. Entry occurs when prices drift below the right-edge high and bottom out, with breakout direction determined by whether prices fall below the formation bottom.

Measure rule - Downward breakout: subtract scallop height from the lowest low
Result is the minimum expected price target
